content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax {916) 322•0886 
Matthew E. Richardson 
City Attorney 
18101 Von Kannan Avenue, Suite 1000 
Irvine, CA 92612 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-212 
Dear Mr. Richardson: 
December 7, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Lake Forest City 
Councilmember Dr. Jim Gardner regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Refonn Act (the "Act")1 and Section l 090. 
This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provis_ions of the Act and Section 1090 and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest. 
When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section 1090, the 
Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the 
local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )(3 ). ) Accordingly, we 
have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District 
Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) 
Please note that the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any 
individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making 
of, and the City Council from entering into, one or more potential contracts with the Lake Forest 
Community Association homeowners association (the "HOA") regarding disputes between the City 
and the HOA relating to a recreational lake ("Lake") owned by the HOA or a pond in Village Pond 
Park ("Pond") that the HOA is contractually obligated to maintain given that the Councilmember is 
a member of the HOA? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 2 
Section 1090 applies to the potential contract or contracts between the City and the HOA 
relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the rule of necessity, the City Council may 
enter into these contracts, but the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the 
making of these contracts, including any negotiations or discussions involving the disputes. 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Lake Forest and the authorized representative of 
the Councilmember. The HOA is a common interest development and a 50l(c)(4) nonprofit 
organization. The HOA is within the City's boundaries. 
The HOA owns common area property that the HOA operates and maintains, including the 
Lake. In February of 2014, the HOA submitted a claim ("HOA claim") for damages against the 
City alleging that rock, soil, and other debris were transported through the City's storm drain 
system and deposited into the Lake necessitating dredging. The City rejected the HOA claim. In 
June of 2014, the City issued a Notice of Violation/Administrative Compliance Order ("City 
citation") to the HOA alleging, among other things, that the HOA had made illegal discharges and 
illicit connections to the City's storm drain system. The HOA timely appealed the City citation. 
The City and HOA executed a tolling agreement in October 2014 that preserves each party's right 
to file a lawsuit or administrative claim against the other party while the parties attempted to 
collaboratively resolve the HOA claim and the City citation. 
Additionally, the HOA is obligated to maintain the water quality of the Pond under the 
Village Pond Park contract between it and the City. In April 2012, the HOA sent the City a letter 
asserting that the HOA should be relieved of its obligation to maintain the water quality of the 
Pond. For the past several years, the parties have been discussing a possible amendment to the 
Village Pond Park contract to alter the parties' responsibilities and financial obligations related to 
the Pond. 
Because the parties have been unable to reach an agreement on these matters, there is a 
growing possibility that the parties will initiate litigation. As a result, future City Council closed 
session meetings may include discussions on settlement negotiations or litigation strategy pertaining 
to these disputes. 
The Councilmember is a member of the HOA, owns his residence within the area of the 
HOA, has interests in both the Lake and the HOA's contractual obligation to maintain the water 
quality of the Pond, and pays dues to the HOA that are determined based on the HOA's expenses. 
The Councilmember's wife is a Director on the HOA 's Board of Directors, and on Friday, 
November 13, 2015, you confirmed over the telephone that the Councilmember's wife is not 
compensated for her service to the HOA. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey (l980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies even when the terms of the proposed contract are demonstrably fair and 
equitable, or are plainly to the public entity's advantage. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) Courts have 
recognized that Section 1090's prohibition must be broadly construed and strictly enforced. (Stigall 
v. City of Taft, supra, at pp. 569-571; Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 579-580; 
City Council v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.) An important, prophylactic statute such 
as Section I 090 should be construed broadly to close loopholes, and should not be constricted and 
enfeebled. (Carson Redevelopmelll Agency v. Padilla (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1334.) 
We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of 
interest under Section 1090. 
Step One: Is the Councilmcmbcr subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. Because the Councilmember is a local officer for the City of 
Lake Forest, he is subject to Section 1090. 
Step Two: Docs the decision at issue involve a contract or contracts? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view 
the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract."' 
(People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.) 
As a general rule, a settlement agreement is treated as a contract. (See, e.g., T.M. Cobb Co. 
V. Superior Court (1984) 36 Cal.3d 273, 280; Weddington Productions, Inc. v. Flick ( 1998) 60. 
Cal.App.4th 793, 810.) Courts have also characterized stipulated judgments or consent judgments 
as having the attributes of contracts. (See Pardee Construction Co. v. City of Camarillo (1984) 37 
Cal.3d 465, 471; Mendly v. County of Los Angeles ( 1994) 25 Cal.App.4th 1193, 1206; U.S. Capital 
Corp. v. Nickelber,y (1981) 120 Cal.App.3d 864, 867.) Moreover, the Attorney General has opined 
on multiple occasions that Section 1090 would prohibit a public official from participating in, or the 
multi-member body of which the official is a member from entering into, a settlement agreement in 
which the official is financially interested. (91 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 (2008); 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
142 (2003).) 


File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 4 
The decisions at issue regarding the Lake are whether to extend the tolling agreement and 
whether to settle or litigate the dispute between the City and the HOA regarding the City citation 
and the HOA claim; the decision at issue regarding the Pond is whether to settle or litigate the 
dispute between the City and the HOA regarding the HOA's responsibilities and financial 
obligations under the Village Pond Park contract. The tolling agreement is a contract. Consistent 
with the general rule and the Attorney General opinions discussed above, we find that a settlement 
agreement resolving the Lake dispute or a settlement agreement resolving the Pond dispute would 
also be contracts. Therefore, these decisions relating to the Lake and the Pond all involve contracts. 
Step Three: Is the Councilmcmber making or participating in making a contract or 
contracts? 
Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who actually execute the contract. Section I 090 
casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract. 
(People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046ó 1052.) Therefore, for purposes of Section 1090, 
participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary 
discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, 
and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 
Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also People 1'. Honig, supra, at p. 329; Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 
569.) 
In order to avoid litigation, the City and the HOA would be required to extend the tolling 
agreement, or to settle the Lake and Pond disputes, and either alternative involves the making of 
one or more contracts. Generally, settlement negotiations and the discussion of potential litigation 
are inextricable. Thus, the Councilmember's participation in any City Council deliberation or 
discussion regarding the tolling agreement, the Lake dispute, or the Pond dispute would constitute 
the making or participation in the making of a contract for purposes of Section l 090. 
Step Four: Docs the Councilmember have a financial interest in the contract(s)? 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 1090 does not 
specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that 
prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or 
the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 
645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
Furthermore, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the term 
"financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 
141, 146 ["(h)owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the 
forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) The 
phrase "financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public official's 
fortunes to the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopmenl Agency v. Padilla, supra, at 
p. 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is 


File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 5 
diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he 
acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
Tolling Agreement: The tolling agreement allows the City and the HOA to preserve each 
party's right to file a lawsuit or administrative claim against the other party while the parties work 
collaboratively to resolve their dispute regarding the City citation and the HOA claim. The 
agreement enables the parties to temporarily avoid litigation and provides them the opportunity to 
completely avoid it. Additional costs and potential new liabilities for both parties come concomitant 
with litigation of the disputes. If these additional costs are borne by the HOA, they will pass 
through to the HOA's members through increased dues. Because the Councilmember is a member 
of the HOA, he is financially interested in the tolling agreement. 
Lake Dispute: If the City and the HOA are unable to settle the Lake dispute over the City 
citation and the HOA claim, both parties will incur additional costs resultant from litigation, and 
these costs will be passed on to HOA members in the fonn of increased dues. The Councilmember 
owns his residence within the area of the HOA and has an ownership interest in the Lake, and the 
value of these interests may decrease if potential buyers are deterred by the uncertainty of ongoing 
litigation between the City and the HOA and the prospect of additional HOA fees. Therefore, the 
Councilmember is financially interested in any contract settling the Lake dispute. 
Pond Dispute: The HOA is responsible for maintaining the water quality of the Pond, and 
the parties have been discussing alteration of the HOA's responsibilities and financial obligations 
under the Village Pond Park contract for several years. If the City and the HOA are unable to settle 
the Pond dispute, both parties will incur additional costs resultant from litigation, and the HOA 's 
portion of these costs will be borne by HOA members in the fonn of increased dues. Therefore, the 
Councilmember is also financially interested in any contract settling the Pond dispute. 
Wife's Volunteer Work as a Director on the HOA 's Board of Directors: The 
Councilmember's wife is a volunteer Director for the HOA's Board of Directors. Although she is 
not compensated for her service, a non-compensated officer of a tax-exempt corporation is 
generally regarded as having at least a remote interest in the contracts of the corporation, and an 
official generally has a financial interest in his or her spouse's financial interests. Thus, the 
Councilmember is also financially interested in these contracts based on his wife being a volunteer 
Director for the HOA's Board of Directors. 
Step Five: Docs either a remote or nonintercst exception apply? 
As a general rule, when Section t 090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member 
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson v. Call, 
supra, at pp. 647-649; Stiga/1 v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 
(2003); 70 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987).) However, the Legislature has created various 
statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed to 
be a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5, or a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091. 


File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 6 
If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the official's abstention, and 
generally a noninterest does not require disclosure. ( City of Vernon v. Central Basi11 Mun. Water 
Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).) If a 
"remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if (1) the official in question discloses his or 
her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted in the entity's 
official records, and (3) the official abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. 
(Section l 091 (a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) 
Only two exceptions merit discussion here, the "noninterest" set forth in Section 
1091.5(a)(8) and the "remote interest" specified in Section 109l(b)(]). While these exceptions 
generally apply to the officers of nonprofit or tax exempt corporations, we have previously advised 
that the exceptions also apply to the official where the official "stands in the shoes of his spouse." 
(See lindgre11 Advice Letter, No. A-15-059; Craft Advice Letter, No. A-14-168; Glover Advice 
Letter, No. A-14-138.) 
We first consider the applicability of the noninterest exception in Section 1091.5(a)(8). 
Section I 091.5(a)(8) provides that an officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a 
contract if his or her interest is: 
"(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit tax-exempt corporation, 
which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or 
board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular 
consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted in its official 
records.'' 
Although the Councilmember's spouse is a volunteer Director on the HOA 's Board of 
Directors, supporting the functions of the City Council is not a primary purpose of the HOA, and 
the City Council is not legally obligated to give the HOA any particular consideration. Therefore, 
this noninterest exception is not applicable to the interests at hand. 
We next consider the applicability of the remote interest exception in Section 1091(b)(I). 
Section l 091 (b )(I) provides that an officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract if his 
or her interest is "[t]hat of an officer or employee of a . . .  nonprofit corporation." Pursuant to this 
exception, the Councilmember's interest resulting from his wife's board position with the HOA is 
only a remote interest. 
Nonetheless, the Councilmember's interest resulting from his wife's board positon with the 
HOA, is not the Councilmember's only financial interest implicated by the contracts at issue. As 
discussed above, the Councilmember is also financially interested in those contracts due to their 
potential effects on his real property interest in his residence, his ownership interest in the Lake, his 
share of the contractual obligation to maintain the water quality of the Pond, and his share of the 
contractual liability for the expenses of the HOA owed in the form of dues. The remote interest 
exception set forth in Section 1091 (b)( I) does not apply to the Councilmember's other financial 
interests in the contracts at issue. 


Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
File No. A-1 5-212 
Page No. 7 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1 090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 1 10 (2005).) The rule of necessity has two facets: in procurement 
situations, it has permitted a government agency to acquire an essential supply or service despite a 
conflict of interest; in nonprocurement situations, it has permitted a public officer to carry out the 
essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one who may 
legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 3 1 0  ( 1982).) In nonprocurement situations, such as the 
situation here, the rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even 
where a conflict of interest exists. (Ibid.) 
Previously, the rule of necessity has been applied in a nonprocurement situation to a city 
council where members of the council had a conflict of interest that otherwise would have 
prevented action by the board. (Federal Construction Co. v. Curd, 179 Cal. 489, 493 ( 1918). In a 
nonprocurement situation where the rule of necessity applies to allow a multi-member body to act 
when it otherwise would have been precluded from doing so due to one or more members' conflict 
of interest, the member or members with the conflict of interest must abstain from participation. (88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. I 06, 1 1 1  (2005); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 1 12 ( 1986); 67 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
369, 378 (1984).) 
Thus, to determine if the rule of necessity applies, we must examine whether resolving these 
disputes is an essential duty of the City Council and whether the City Council is the only 
government entity legally capable of settling or litigating these disputes. 
The City is a general law city, and its City Council is responsible for making policy 
decisions relating to the City and its residents. Furthermore, Lake Forest Municipal Code Section 
2.08.060 grants the City Council ultimate authority over the City's budget. Given its broad authority 
over the City's policies and budget, addressing and resolving the Lake and Pond disputes is an 
essential duty of the City Council. If the City Council was precluded from settling or litigating these 
disputes while the Councilmember is in office, no other government entity would be able to act on 
these disputes without the City experiencing loss of due process, significant delay, or increased 
costs. Therefore, the City Council is the only government entity legally capable of settling or 
litigating these disputes. 
In light of the requirement that the conflicted member abstain if the rule of necessity applies, 
there is simply no interest served by prohibiting the City from addressing and resolving these 
disputes. According) y, we find that the rule of necessity applies to the potential contracts at issue. 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section I 090 applies to any potential 
contract between the City and the HOA relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the 
rule of necessity, the City Council may enter into one or more of these potential contracts, but the 
Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the making of these contracts. 2 
2 You have also asked whether the Act would prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to the tolling agreement, the Lake dispute, and the Pond dispute. Because Section 1090 and the rule of 
necessity apply to those potential contracL,;, the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in making 


File No. A-15-212 
Page No. 8 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (91 6) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
those contracts. Therefore, we do not address your question regarding the Act further because the requirements of the 
Act would also be satisfied by the Councilmember's abstention. We do note, however, that the Act sets forth specific 
procedures for a public official to properly recuse him or herself if the official is disqualified from participating in a 
governmental decision due to a conflict of interest (Section 87105; Regulation 18707).

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making 
of, and the City Council from entering into, one or more potential contracts with the Lake Forest 
Community Association homeowners association (the "HOA") regarding disputes between the City 
and the HOA relating to a recreational lake ("Lake") owned by the HOA or a pond in Village Pond 
Park ("Pond") that the HOA is contractually obligated to maintain given that the Councilmember is 
a member of the HOA? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in
sections.conclusion:
Section 1090 applies to the potential contract or contracts between the City and the HOA 
relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the rule of necessity, the City Council may 
enter into these contracts, but the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the 
making of these contracts, including any negotiations or discussions involving the disputes.

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their

A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view

(People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.)

142 (2003).)  
The decisions at issue regarding the Lake are whether to extend the tolling agreement and

and the HOA claim; the decision at issue regarding the Pond is whether to settle or litigate the 
dispute between the City and the HOA regarding the HOA's responsibilities and financial

contracts? 
Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who actually execute the contract. Section I 090

569.)

Step Four: Docs the Councilmember have a financial interest in the contract(s)? 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 1090 does not 
specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that 
prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or 
the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 
645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
Furthermore, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the term 
"financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 
141, 146 ["(h)owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the 
forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) The 
phrase "financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public official's 
fortunes to the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopmenl Agency v. Padilla, supra, at 
p. 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is  
diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he 
acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
Tolling Agreement: The tolling agreement allows the City and the HOA to preserve each 
party's right to file a lawsuit or administrative claim against the other party while the parties work 
collaboratively to resolve their dispute regarding the City citation and the HOA claim. The 
agreement enables the parties to temporarily avoid litigation and provides them the opportunity to 
completely avoid it. Additional costs and potential new liabilities for both parties come concomitant 
with litigation of the disputes. If these additional costs are borne by the HOA, they will pass 
through to the HOA's members through increased dues. Because the Councilmember is a member 
of the HOA, he is financially interested in the tolling agreement. 
Lake Dispute: If the City and the HOA are unable to settle the Lake dispute over the City 
citation and the HOA claim, both parties will incur additional costs resultant from litigation, and 
these costs will be passed on to HOA members in the fonn of increased dues. The Councilmember 
owns his residence within the area of the HOA and has an ownership interest in the Lake, and the 
value of these interests may decrease if potential buyers are deterred by the uncertainty of ongoing 
litigation between the City and the HOA and the prospect of additional HOA fees. Therefore, the 
Councilmember is financially interested in any contract settling the Lake dispute. 
Pond Dispute: The HOA is responsible for maintaining the water quality of the Pond, and 
the parties have been discussing alteration of the HOA's responsibilities and financial obligations 
under the Village Pond Park contract for several years. If the City and the HOA are unable to settle 
the Pond dispute, both parties will incur additional costs resultant from litigation, and the HOA 's 
portion of these costs will be borne by HOA members in the fonn of increased dues. Therefore, the 
Councilmember is also financially interested in any contract settling the Pond dispute. 
Wife's Volunteer Work as a Director on the HOA 's Board of Directors: The 
Councilmember's wife is a volunteer Director for the HOA's Board of Directors. Although she is 
not compensated for her service, a non-compensated officer of a tax-exempt corporation is 
generally regarded as having at least a remote interest in the contracts of the corporation, and an 
official generally has a financial interest in his or her spouse's financial interests. Thus, the 
Councilmember is also financially interested in these contracts based on his wife being a volunteer 
Director for the HOA's Board of Directors. 
Step Five: Docs either a remote or nonintercst exception apply? 
As a general rule, when Section t 090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member 
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson v. Call, 
supra, at pp. 647-649; Stiga/1 v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 
(2003); 70 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987).) However, the Legislature has created various 
statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed to 
be a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5, or a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091.  
If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the official's abstention, and 
generally a noninterest does not require disclosure. ( City of Vernon v. Central Basi11 Mun. Water 
Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).) If a 
"remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if (1) the official in question discloses his or 
her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted in the entity's 
official records, and (3) the official abstains from any participation in the making of the contract. 
(Section l 091 (a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) 
Only two exceptions merit discussion here, the "noninterest" set forth in Section 
1091.5(a)(8) and the "remote interest" specified in Section 109l(b)(]). While these exceptions 
generally apply to the officers of nonprofit or tax exempt corporations, we have previously advised 
that the exceptions also apply to the official where the official "stands in the shoes of his spouse." 
(See lindgre11 Advice Letter, No. A-15-059; Craft Advice Letter, No. A-14-168; Glover Advice 
Letter, No. A-14-138.) 
We first consider the applicability of the noninterest exception in Section 1091.5(a)(8). 
Section I 091.5(a)(8) provides that an officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a 
contract if his or her interest is: 
"(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit tax-exempt corporation, 
which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or 
board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular 
consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted in its official 
records.'' 
Although the Councilmember's spouse is a volunteer Director on the HOA 's Board of 
Directors, supporting the functions of the City Council is not a primary purpose of the HOA, and 
the City Council is not legally obligated to give the HOA any particular consideration. Therefore, 
this noninterest exception is not applicable to the interests at hand. 
We next consider the applicability of the remote interest exception in Section 1091(b)(I). 
Section l 091 (b )(I) provides that an officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract if his 
or her interest is "[t]hat of an officer or employee of a . . .  nonprofit corporation." Pursuant to this 
exception, the Councilmember's interest resulting from his wife's board position with the HOA is 
only a remote interest. 
Nonetheless, the Councilmember's interest resulting from his wife's board positon with the 
HOA, is not the Councilmember's only financial interest implicated by the contracts at issue. As 
discussed above, the Councilmember is also financially interested in those contracts due to their 
potential effects on his real property interest in his residence, his ownership interest in the Lake, his 
share of the contractual obligation to maintain the water quality of the Pond, and his share of the 
contractual liability for the expenses of the HOA owed in the form of dues. The remote interest 
exception set forth in Section 1091 (b)( I) does not apply to the Councilmember's other financial 
interests in the contracts at issue. 


Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
File No. A-1 5-212  
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1 090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 1 10 (2005).) The rule of necessity has two facets: in procurement 
situations, it has permitted a government agency to acquire an essential supply or service despite a 
conflict of interest; in nonprocurement situations, it has permitted a public officer to carry out the 
essential duties of the office despite a conflict of interest where the officer is the only one who may 
legally act. (65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 3 1 0  ( 1982).) In nonprocurement situations, such as the 
situation here, the rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even 
where a conflict of interest exists. (Ibid.) 
Previously, the rule of necessity has been applied in a nonprocurement situation to a city 
council where members of the council had a conflict of interest that otherwise would have 
prevented action by the board. (Federal Construction Co. v. Curd, 179 Cal. 489, 493 ( 1918). In a 
nonprocurement situation where the rule of necessity applies to allow a multi-member body to act 
when it otherwise would have been precluded from doing so due to one or more members' conflict 
of interest, the member or members with the conflict of interest must abstain from participation. (88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. I 06, 1 1 1  (2005); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 1 12 ( 1986); 67 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
369, 378 (1984).) 
Thus, to determine if the rule of necessity applies, we must examine whether resolving these 
disputes is an essential duty of the City Council and whether the City Council is the only 
government entity legally capable of settling or litigating these disputes. 
The City is a general law city, and its City Council is responsible for making policy 
decisions relating to the City and its residents. Furthermore, Lake Forest Municipal Code Section 
2.08.060 grants the City Council ultimate authority over the City's budget. Given its broad authority 
over the City's policies and budget, addressing and resolving the Lake and Pond disputes is an 
essential duty of the City Council. If the City Council was precluded from settling or litigating these 
disputes while the Councilmember is in office, no other government entity would be able to act on 
these disputes without the City experiencing loss of due process, significant delay, or increased 
costs. Therefore, the City Council is the only government entity legally capable of settling or 
litigating these disputes. 
In light of the requirement that the conflicted member abstain if the rule of necessity applies, 
there is simply no interest served by prohibiting the City from addressing and resolving these 
disputes. According) y, we find that the rule of necessity applies to the potential contracts at issue. 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section I 090 applies to any potential 
contract between the City and the HOA relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the 
rule of necessity, the City Council may enter into one or more of these potential contracts, but the 
Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the making of these contracts. 2 
2 You have also asked whether the Act would prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to the tolling agreement, the Lake dispute, and the Pond dispute. Because Section 1090 and the rule of 
necessity apply to those potential contracL,;, the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in making

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making 
of, and the City Council from entering into, one or more potential contracts with the Lake Forest 
Community Association homeowners association (the "HOA") regarding disputes between the City 
and the HOA relating to a recreational lake ("Lake") owned by the HOA or a pond in Village Pond 
Park ("Pond") that the HOA is contractually obligated to maintain given that the Councilmember is 
a member of the HOA? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: Section 1090 applies to the potential contract or contracts between the City and the HOA 
relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the rule of necessity, the City Council may 
enter into these contracts, but the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the 
making of these contracts, including any negotiations or discussions involving the disputes.

Richardson City Attorney 18101 Von Kannan Avenue, Suite 1000 Irvine, CA 92612 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-15-212 Dear Mr. Richardson: December 7, 2015 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Lake Forest City Councilmember Dr. Jim Gardner regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act")1 and Section l 090. This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provis_ions of the Act and Section 1090 and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section 1090, the Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )(3 ). ) Accordingly, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) Please note that the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, and the City Council from entering into, one or more potential contracts with the Lake Forest Community Association homeowners association (the "HOA") regarding disputes between the City and the HOA relating to a recreational lake ("Lake") owned by the HOA or a pond in Village Pond Park ("Pond") that the HOA is contractually obligated to maintain given that the Councilmember is a member of the HOA? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSION File No. A-15-212 Page No. 2 Section 1090 applies to the potential contract or contracts between the City and the HOA relating to the Lake and Pond disputes. However, under the rule of necessity, the City Council may enter into these contracts, but the Councilmember must abstain from making or participating in the making of these contracts, including any negotiations or discussions involving the disputes. FACTS You are the City Attorney for the City of Lake Forest and the authorized representative of the Councilmember. The HOA is a common interest
